definition: The correlation sign between stocks and bonds is structurally determined by the relative variance of demand versus supply shocks, filtered through the central bank reaction function. When demand shocks dominate, countercyclical monetary policy drives negative correlation; when supply shocks dominate, simultaneous discount-rate repricing drives positive correlation.
evidence: Campbell-Sunderam-Viceira (2017) empirical anchor
NK three-equation model derivation (Galí 2015)
2022: S&P 500 EPS grew ~4% yet index fell ~19% on P/E compression while bonds lost ~13%
Current shock mix estimated ~55-60% supply-driven, down from ~75% in 2022
Survived all four debate rounds without challenge
open_questions: How to measure the demand/supply shock mix in real time with precision
Whether AI-driven productivity gains shift the mix toward demand-dominated

definition: The current stock-bond correlation regime is transitional and oscillating rather than locked into persistent positive territory. Co-present demand shocks (fiscal support) and supply shocks (tariffs, reshoring) are roughly balanced, producing bifurcated and unstable correlation readings.
evidence: All eight agents independently converge on this characterization
August 2024 growth scare produced temporary negative correlation
Q1 2025 tariff volatility produced correlation reversal
Dollar remaining range-bound rather than confirming regime-shift conjunction
Mega-cap tech echoes 1990s while broader market echoes 1970s
open_questions: Whether oscillation constitutes a permanent third regime or a transition
Duration of oscillation phase — could persist through 2027 at 40% probability

definition: A structurally meaningful bifurcation exists where 2Y-SPX correlation is negative (reflecting still-credible monetary expectations channel) while 30Y-SPX correlation is positive (reflecting fiscal supply dynamics outside the central bank toolkit).
evidence: Five agents independently document and interpret the bifurcation
Short end reflects Taylor Rule expectations (demand-responsive)
Long end reflects fiscal supply dynamics (demand-independent)
Near-term earnings (25-30% of equity PV) track 2Y; terminal value (55-65%) tracks 30Y
August 2024 growth scare briefly restored negative correlation at all maturities
open_questions: Is this structural (fiscal dominance) or transient (QT + supply/demand dynamics)
N=1 episode over ~3 years — can the Fed simultaneously be credible and not credible
Falsifiable test: if 2Y-SPX turns positive, it signals full transmission breakdown

definition: The fiscal trajectory is a critical, co-equal determinant of the correlation regime alongside the central bank reaction function. Fiscal balance/GDP explains ~35-40% of cross-country variation in correlation sign after controlling for inflation.
evidence: Cross-country panel regressions (Ang & Longstaff 2013, Viceira et al. 2021)
Japan: fiscal supply shifted long-end correlation positive even under deflation
Australia: small deficits + credible RBA = most persistently negative correlation in G10
UK mini-budget 2022: gilt-FTSE correlation spiked to +0.60-0.70 from fiscal shock alone
$2T+/year US fiscal deficit at 6-7% GDP with no consolidation path through 2028
Geopolitical imperatives (defense, CHIPS Act) create non-discretionary spending
open_questions: Does the correlation regime itself affect the fiscal path (doom loop vs self-correction)
At what deficit/GDP threshold does fiscal dominance overwhelm monetary credibility
Can AI productivity gains substitute for fiscal consolidation in regime resolution

definition: Positive stock-bond correlation does not imply negative portfolio returns. 60/40 portfolios returned approximately +12.1% (2023), +14.3% (2024), and ~+8% (2025 est.) during a widely characterized positive correlation regime. Positive correlation only catastrophically fails during stagflation.
evidence: Positive correlation with disinflation + growth resilience is benign for 60/40
Consensus conflates correlation SIGN with return SIGN
Pair_3 debate labeled this 'basic but devastating to the consensus narrative'
No agent contested this claim
open_questions: Whether the current benign outcome lulls investors into complacency before a stagflationary shock
How long the positive-correlation-positive-return configuration can persist

definition: The 1993-94 bond massacre is the highest-similarity historical analogue for the current environment, sharing aggressive Fed tightening demonstrating credibility, declining-but-above-comfort inflation, maturity bifurcation, and oscillating correlation. The 1970s analogue is misleading due to absent structural preconditions.
evidence: Four agents independently converge on 1993-94 as best starting-point analogue
1970s structural preconditions absent: 30%+ unionization then vs ~10% now, oil monopoly vs fragmented market
5Y5Y breakevens anchored at 2.2-2.5% vs unanchored expectations in 1970s
Base rate of positive correlation episodes becoming 1970s-like is ~1/6-7 since 1970
Pair_3 debate confirmed 1970s analogue as 'decisively weaker'
open_questions: Today has Phase 1 of the 1994-98 resolution but lacks Phases 2-5
Whether the analogue breaks down if fiscal deficits remain above 5% GDP

definition: The Taylor Rule coefficient on inflation (φ_π) is the institutional lynchpin of the correlation regime. φ_π < 1 generates indeterminacy and positive correlation; φ_π > 1.5 generates determinacy and negative correlation; 1.0-1.5 is the danger zone.
evidence: NK model derivation (Clarida-Galí-Gertler framework)
Fed 525bp hiking cycle (2022-23) demonstrated φ_π ≈ 1.8-2.0
Current easing phase suggests φ_π ≈ 1.3-1.7 (transition zone)
Fed tightened aggressively against political pressure — strong revealed-preference evidence
Historical precedent: Fed cut in 2001 despite 2.6% core CPI, in 2020 despite incipient inflation
open_questions: Will the Fed cut aggressively in next recession even if inflation is moderately above target
Whether political pressure eventually erodes φ_π below 1.0

definition: SPX at 21-22x forward P/E implicitly prices negative correlation (ERP ~4.5-5.0% consistent with bonds-as-hedge). Under persistent positive correlation, required ERP rises 50-100bp, implying fair value of 18-19x — a 10-15% gap. Credit spreads add a third inconsistency.
evidence: IG OAS ~90-110bp implies near-zero recession probability
Equity valuations lagged correlation regime shifts by 18-36 months in every prior transition
Nifty Fifty valuations persisted 4+ years into 1968 positive regime
Pair_0 debate concluded mispricing is real but should not be expected to resolve quickly
Three resolution paths: equities derate, bonds rally confirming equity bet, or inconsistency persists
open_questions: Whether different investor bases with different models can sustain the inconsistency indefinitely
Timing of resolution — base rate suggests 18-36 month lag

definition: Vol-targeting and risk-parity strategies ($500B-$1T AUM) create a structural amplification mechanism for correlation regime transitions through a reflexive loop: correlation rise → portfolio vol rise → forced selling of both stocks and bonds → actual correlation increase.
evidence: Basis trade ($800B-$1T+ notional at 50-100x leverage) amplifies forced Treasury selling
October 2023 (30Y to ~5.0%) as partial preview
Pair_0 debate refuted 1987 portfolio insurance timing analogue
4 years into transition without major cascade — unclear if risk is diminishing or building
open_questions: Timing and threshold for potential cascade remain genuinely unknown
Whether endogenous deleveraging creates growth scares that restore negative correlation (self-correcting)

definition: A discrete stress event demonstrating bonds' safe-haven function is likely required for durable negative correlation reassertion. All seven catalogued G10 transitions from positive to negative correlation required a discrete crisis; the reverse transition emerged gradually.
evidence: US 1998 (LTCM/Russia), UK 2001 (tech bust), Germany 2002 (Worldcom), Australia 2000 (tech bust)
Behavioral mechanism: investors learn hedging property by experiencing it during acute stress
Asymmetric transition dynamics: positive→negative requires crisis, negative→positive is gradual
Rated 'single most valuable empirical contribution' in pair_0 debate
open_questions: Whether gradual inflation moderation alone could substitute for a crystallizing event
What type and magnitude of crisis would be sufficient

definition: The conditional base rate for persistent (>5 year) positive correlation is only 25-40%, not the 70-85% implied by consensus positioning. Under moderate declining inflation with fiscal deficits below 5% GDP, negative correlation reassertion rate is ~75-80%. Above 5% GDP deficits, the sample drops to n=2 with ambiguous results.
evidence: Rolling 12-month correlation crossed zero 8-10 times since 1990; only 1-2 were persistent regime shifts
Unconditional base rate ~25-35% for persistence
Fiscal conditioning variable separates clear from ambiguous historical episodes
Current 6-7% deficit places us in genuinely uncertain territory
Bayesian calculation 'methodologically sound but practically misleading' per pair_3 debate
open_questions: Whether zero-crossings can be treated as drawn from the same distribution
How to update the base rate given the unprecedented fiscal deficit context

definition: Forward guidance transmission is attenuated by approximately 30-50% under positive correlation. Under negative correlation, dovish guidance eases through both bond and equity channels simultaneously (amplifying 1.5-2x); under positive correlation, channels partially offset.
evidence: Del Negro, Giannoni & Patterson (2023) estimate 30-50% attenuation
R-squared of FOMC-day yield changes on equity returns declined from 0.15-0.25 to 0.05-0.10
Bond channel still functions mechanically — attenuation, not collapse
Original 'near-zero' claim from rates_strategist_02 refuted in pair_2 debate
open_questions: Whether attenuation is stable or varies with correlation magnitude
Implications for next easing cycle effectiveness

definition: ROIC-WACC spread compression from ~6-8pp (2020-21) to ~4-5pp currently creates nonlinear equity sensitivity to rate changes. At a 4pp spread, a 1pp WACC increase destroys ~25% of economic profit vs ~10% at a 10pp spread, making the equity market structurally more rate-sensitive.
evidence: Mathematical relationship is definitional (not model-dependent)
Pair_1 debate rated this 'genuinely useful reframing'
Rates now damage equity fundamentals (earnings) not just valuations (P/E)
Next equity downturn potentially more rate-sensitive than any since 1970s
open_questions: Whether mega-cap tech with high ROIC is immune to this dynamic
How quickly ROIC-WACC spreads can re-expand in a rate-cutting cycle

definition: The $2.5T corporate debt maturity wall (2025-2027) acts as a structural dual-channel amplifier of positive correlation. Refinancing from 2-3% to 5.5-6.0% coupons creates ~$50-75B incremental annual interest expense (~$2.50-3.75 S&P 500 EPS headwind) through the earnings channel, additive to the valuation channel.
evidence: Interest expense/EBIT for S&P 500 ex-mega-cap rose from ~12% (2021) to ~18% (2025E)
Tracking toward ~22-25% by 2027 at current rates
Well-documented by rating agencies and credit research
Not contested in debate
open_questions: Whether companies can sufficiently deleverage before refinancing
Impact on credit spreads if earnings deteriorate simultaneously

definition: Portfolio construction should shift 10-15% from nominal duration toward convexity-based hedging including trend-following (CTA), long volatility, and real assets (TIPS, gold, commodity producers). Under positive/unstable correlation, only strategies with positive convexity in both tails provide genuine diversification.
evidence: 2022: SG CTA Index +20% while 60/40 lost ~16%
TIPS at 2.3-2.5% real yield provide inflation protection
Contrarian TLT call overlay exploits elevated put skew
Ordinal ranking (short-duration > long-duration equities, real > nominal bonds) is high conviction
10-15% reallocation magnitude is 'a judgment call, not a derived optimum'
open_questions: Execution details add 200-300bp uncertainty to Sharpe estimates
Whether CTA strategies remain effective if correlation oscillation becomes the norm

definition: Non-GAAP earnings practices systematically understate equity market duration by approximately 30-40%. S&P 500 GAAP EPS (~$200-210) vs non-GAAP (~$250-260) creates a ~25% gap driven primarily by SBC exclusion (~$180-200B annually), hiding positive-correlation exposure from standard risk models.
evidence: At GAAP multiples (26-29x), 55-65% of PV in terminal value with ~15-20% sensitivity per 100bp
At non-GAAP multiples (21-22x), 45-50% terminal value with ~12-15% sensitivity
Pair_1 debate rated this 'genuinely original'
Direction 'almost certainly correct' but 30-40% magnitude is author's construction
open_questions: Needs wider empirical study — magnitude is author's construction not widely validated
Whether risk models will incorporate this adjustment

definition: Earnings quality screening provides amplified downside protection (3-5x) under positive correlation, functioning as a primary hedge rather than merely an alpha source. The correlation regime systematically signals transition from growth-rewarded to cash-conversion-rewarded markets.
evidence: 2022: S&P 500 Quality Index sleeve drew down ~-11% vs standard ~-16% vs ARK-style ~-45%
Quality spread ~34pp in 2022 vs typical ~5-8pp in normal years (4-5x amplification)
Pair_1 debate: 'most actionable portfolio construction insight'
N=1 limitation — requires multi-cycle confirmation
open_questions: Whether the 3-5x amplification is specific to 2022 or generalizable across positive correlation episodes
Systematic backtesting across full 1960s-2020s correlation history needed

definition: QE/QT operates as an independent correlation channel distinct from the policy rate. QE removes duration from the private sector (correlation-suppressive); QT restores it (correlation-promoting). QT cessation (late 2026-2027) is a time-bound catalyst for correlation stabilization.
evidence: ~$1.8-2.0T shed from peak Fed balance sheet
Fed cannot simultaneously tighten via QT and ease via rate cuts without contradictory signals
BOJ YCC case as extreme supporting evidence
Quantification of QT's specific contribution is model-dependent
open_questions: How much QT specifically contributes to positive correlation vs fiscal supply and inflation uncertainty
Whether QT cessation alone is sufficient or merely necessary for stabilization

definition: Cross-country evidence from Japan, Australia, and UK provides natural experiments confirming the fiscal-monetary policy mix as a separable, co-equal correlation driver. Each country isolates a different channel from inflation effects.
evidence: Japan: fiscal supply shifted long-end correlation positive even under deflation (isolating fiscal channel)
Australia: small deficits + credible RBA = most persistently negative G10 correlation
UK mini-budget 2022: gilt-FTSE spiked to +0.60-0.70 from fiscal shock at same inflation as US
Ang & Longstaff (2013) and Viceira et al. (2021) panel regression support
open_questions: Whether institutional differences limit cross-country applicability to the US
How to weight fiscal vs monetary channels when they conflict

definition: The 1994-98 five-phase resolution sequence (term premium shock → fiscal consolidation → EM crisis → LTCM safe-haven validation → productivity boom) resolved the 1993-94 bifurcation into durable negative correlation. Today has Phase 1 only; Phase 2 (fiscal consolidation) is unavailable at 6-7% GDP deficits.
evidence: Each phase is historically documented with specific dates and mechanisms
Without Phase 2, Phases 3-5 cannot replicate even if catalysts emerge
Pair_0 debate endorsed as 'most rigorous use of analogue reasoning'
No political pathway to deficits below 4% GDP before 2028
open_questions: Whether an alternative resolution sequence exists that bypasses fiscal consolidation
Could AI productivity boom substitute for fiscal consolidation (Phase 5 without Phase 2)

definition: The probability-weighted regime outlook assigns roughly 35-45% to continued oscillation, 25-35% to negative reassertion (recession-dependent), and 15-25% to persistent positive (fiscal dominance dependent). The distribution is bimodal with fat tails.
evidence: Three agents provide independent distributions that roughly converge
macro_strategist_01: 40% oscillating, 30% negative, 20% positive, 10% recession snap-back
generalist_02: 35% deflationary recession, 20% instability, 20% Treasury dislocation, 15% inflation re-acceleration
Debates refuted specific probability point estimates as carrying false precision (±10-15pp)
Ordering and bimodal structure survive; precise numbers are illustrative ranges
open_questions: Whether oscillation is the true modal outcome or an artifact of model uncertainty
How to update probabilities as fiscal and monetary data evolve

definition: The recession trigger type determines the correlation sign: Fed-triggered recessions produce immediate negative correlation (3/3 base rate); exogenous-shock recessions may produce initial positive correlation (2/5 cases) before the Fed response shifts it negative within 1-3 months. Overall 7/8 hit rate for eventual negative.
evidence: Systematic analysis across 8 post-war US recessions
Fed-triggered (1970, 1980, 1982): 3/3 immediate negative
Exogenous-shock (1974, 1990, 2001, 2008, 2020): initial positive in 2/5
Fed rate-cutting response is the most powerful negative-correlation mechanism
open_questions: Recession trigger type is rarely identifiable in advance
Whether the next recession would be Fed-triggered or exogenous-shock

definition: Financial conditions indices systematically mismeasure the policy stance under positive correlation, understating tightening by the equivalent of the portfolio vol increase (~40-46%). 60/40 vol rises from ~8.4% (ρ=-0.30) to ~12.3% (ρ=+0.30), triggering deleveraging not captured by standard FCI measures.
evidence: 46% portfolio vol increase arithmetic is uncontested
Triggers VaR-based deleveraging, risk-parity rebalancing, pension ALM pressure
Not captured by Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI, or Bloomberg FCI
Pair_2 debate upgraded assessment: 'logic is tighter than confidence score reflected'
open_questions: Whether the Fed accounts for this mismeasurement informally
Precise magnitude of mismeasurement vs its existence

definition: The maturity bifurcation can resolve via four paths: fiscal consolidation (~10% probability), central bank backstop (~30%), inflation de-anchoring (~15%), or managed financial repression (~30%). The two most likely paths both involve central bank accommodation of fiscal dynamics.
evidence: Path A: US 1994-98, Canada 1995-97
Path B: ECB OMT 2012, BoE Sept 2022, Fed March 2020
Path C: Brazil 2015-16, Turkey 2018-19
Path D: Japan 2003 VaR shock → YCC, Italy 2012 pre-OMT
Both most-likely paths consistent with fiscal dominance findings from iter_0016
open_questions: Whether a fifth path exists combining elements of multiple resolution types
Timeline for resolution — could extend beyond 2028

definition: The dollar cycle determines whether a positive correlation episode is transient or persistent. The three-factor conjunction model requires inflation expectations + term premium + dollar breakdown simultaneously for a durable regime shift. The 2022-25 dollar strength (DXY 100-107) explains oscillation rather than entrenched positive correlation.
evidence: 1971-78: dollar decline + persistent positive correlation
1994: dollar stable + transient positive correlation
2022-25: strong dollar + oscillating correlation matches 'missing third factor' prediction
N=4 regime transitions as empirical base — suggestive but small
open_questions: Whether the three-factor model is overfitted to a small sample
What would trigger the dollar breakdown leg of the conjunction

definition: A potential self-reinforcing doom loop where positive correlation → higher term premium → larger deficits (interest expense) → more long-end pressure → more positive correlation. Alternatively, a self-correcting mechanism where financial conditions tightening slows the economy and reduces deficits through automatic stabilizers.
evidence: Identified by generalist_01 as 'most important unanswered question'
Neither agent in any pair attempted to resolve this
Flagged in pair_0 debate as the critical gap
Direction of feedback loop has massive implications for regime stability
open_questions: Whether the loop is self-reinforcing (explosive) or self-correcting (stable)
Empirical tests to distinguish between doom loop and stabilization dynamics
Time horizon over which the feedback loop operates

definition: S&P 500 index concentration (top 10 = ~35%) potentially distorts measured stock-bond correlation. Mega-cap tech with exceptional ROIC and net-cash balance sheets may mask the broader S&P 493 already being in robustly positive correlation territory.
evidence: equity_analyst_02 bifurcated analogue: mega-cap ≈ 1994-2000, broad market ≈ 1967-1982
Multiple agents raise the issue but none resolve it empirically
No agent provides equal-weight SPX correlation test
Flagged as highest-priority data question for future research
open_questions: Whether equal-weight SPX correlation with bonds looks meaningfully different
How concentration changes over time affect aggregate correlation measurement

definition: Phillips Curve convexity near the zero output gap (CBO estimate -0.3% to +0.3%) makes the current economy maximally sensitive to small shocks, producing outsized correlation swings of 0.15-0.25 in rolling 3-month windows.
evidence: Grounded in Ball-Mankiw-Romer (1988) and Hazell et al. (2022)
Firms adjust prices more aggressively to cost shocks when operating near capacity
Provides structural explanation for observed correlation instability
open_questions: Limited empirical validation at this specific output gap level
Whether the convexity relationship is stable across different inflation regimes

definition: The NAIRU has potentially shifted up 0.3-0.5pp to ~4.3-4.5%, meaning the current 4.1% unemployment rate is tighter than it appears and consistent with above-target wage growth.
evidence: Post-pandemic early retirements (~3.5M excess)
Immigration policy tightening (net migration down from ~3.3M to ~1.0-1.5M)
Outward Beveridge Curve shift
ECI at ~3.8-4.0% (above ~3.0-3.5% consistent with 2% inflation)
Crump et al. (2024, NBER) model-based evidence
open_questions: NAIRU is notoriously hard to estimate in real time (±1pp confidence bands per Staiger-Stock-Watson 1997)
Shift could reverse if immigration policy changes or participation recovers

definition: Geopolitical imperatives (defense spending rising to 2-3%+ GDP, CHIPS Act $280B, EU Chips Act EUR 43B, reshoring) create structural fiscal commitments that are not discretionary, providing a political economy explanation for why fiscal consolidation is unavailable.
evidence: NATO defense spending rising to 2-3%+ GDP
CHIPS Act $280B, EU Chips Act EUR 43B
Pair_3 debate questioned whether this is isolable from standard fiscal/inflation channels
May be better understood as explaining why fiscal consolidation is unavailable rather than as independent correlation driver
open_questions: Whether this constitutes an independent correlation driver or is subsumed by fiscal trajectory
Magnitude of impact relative to standard inflation and monetary channels

definition: Fiscal expansion can simultaneously fail to boost demand (partial Ricardian offset, multiplier 0.5-0.8) while pushing long-end yields higher through term premium from supply absorption — producing unambiguously correlation-positive effects at 10Y+ maturities without corresponding demand-side growth.
evidence: Derived from macro_strategist_01 Ricardian framework
Fiscal purgatory concept: multiplier sustains output without resolution
Explains divergence between short-end and long-end correlation behavior
Consistent with maturity bifurcation observation
open_questions: Whether Ricardian behavior is stable or shifts with fiscal deficit size
How to measure the Ricardian offset in real time

definition: A three-way inconsistency exists across asset classes: equities price negative correlation and no recession (21-22x P/E), bonds price positive correlation and term premium (high yields), and credit prices neither recession nor correlation stress (IG OAS ~90-110bp). This extends beyond the standard stocks-vs-bonds framing.
evidence: Rated 'most original contribution' in pair_0 debate
Each asset class reflects a different investor base with different models
Historical base rate: cross-asset disagreements can persist for years
Resolution requires one or more markets to reprice
open_questions: Which market reprices first and in which direction
Whether the inconsistency is arbitrageable or reflects genuinely different information
